Abstract
--------

Implanted neural interfaces are electronic devices that stimulate or record from neurons with the purpose of improving the quality of life of people who suffer from neural injury or disease. Devices have been designed to interact with neurons throughout the body to treat a growing variety of conditions. The development and use of implanted neural interfaces is increasing steadily and has shown great success, with implants lasting for years to decades and improving the health and quality of life of many patient populations. Despite these successes, implanted neural interfaces face a multitude of challenges to remain effective for the lifetime of their users. The devices are comprised of several electronic and mechanical components that each may be susceptible to failure. Furthermore, implanted neural interfaces, like any foreign body, will evoke an immune response. The immune response will differ for implants in the central nervous system and peripheral nervous system, as well as over time, ultimately resulting in encapsulation of the device. This review describes the challenges faced by developers of neural interface systems, particularly devices already in use in humans. The mechanical and technological failure modes of each component of an implant system is described. The acute and chronic reactions to devices in the peripheral and central nervous system and how they affect system performance are depicted. Further, physical challenges such as micro and macro movements are reviewed. The clinical implications of device failures are summarized and a guide for determining the severity of complication was developed and provided. Common methods to diagnose and examine mechanical, technological, and biological failure modes at various stages of development and testing are outlined, with an emphasis on chronic in vivo characterization of implant systems. Finally, this review concludes with an overview of some of the innovative solutions developed to reduce or resolve the challenges faced by implanted neural interface systems.

**Keywords:** Neural interfaces, Biocompatibility, Medical devices, Chronic in vivo, Bioelectronics, Failure testing

Background
----------

Implanted neural interfaces are electronic devices that interact with the nervous system to sense (record) or stimulate neural activity to treat neurological disorders. They differ slightly from other implanted bioelectronic devices, such as pacemakers, in that they are implanted in or near neural tissue, including the brain, spinal cord, or peripheral nerves (Fig.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#Fig1)). Several neural interfaces for stimulating and recording, such as cochlear implants, deep brain stimulation (DBS), spinal cord stimulation (SCS), electrocorticography (ECoG), and depth electrodes have market approval and have been used clinically for decades with great success. Some of these devices are used clinically “off-label” to explore new treatment applications, and several emerging neural interface technologies are being developed and tested in commercial and research investigational clinical trials (Fig.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#Fig2)). The major components of implanted neural interface systems are: (i) the pulse generator (for stimulation) and/or data acquisition (DAQ) device (for recording), (ii) power and communication, (iii) packaging, (iv), lead wires and interconnects, and (v) electrode(s) (example of a cochlear implant system shown in Fig.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#Fig3)).

### Fig.3.

[![Image 11: Fig. 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/5541/11907899/a1ec5e91bdc0/42234_2025_168_Fig3_HTML.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11907899_42234_2025_168_Fig3_HTML.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/figure/Fig3/)

Cochlear implant, demonstrating the variety of components of implanted neural interfaces. The magnets connect the external (left) and internal (right) units across the skin. The microphone on the external unit records sound, the sound processor converts the sound recordings to a digital signal with stimulation commands, the stimulation commands and power from the battery are sent from the external to the internal transceiver, the stimulation commands are converted into a stimulation output by the pulse generator, the lead wires carry the current to the electrodes, which generate an electric field that activates auditory neurons that lie outside the cochlea. Recording of impedance at the electrode-tissue interface can also occur, where the DAQ records the electrode impedance with reference to the ground electrode, and transmits these data to a different external transceiver to transfer the data to a computer for viewing by clinical team members

Pulse generators deliver electrical current to activate the target neurons. Recording DAQ devices typically contain amplifiers and filters to increase the signal-to-noise ratio of the recorded signal. Both stimulating and recording devices might also have components for additional processing and control. While many clinical devices use fully implantable pulse generators, including SCS and DBS, other devices, such as many models of cochlear implants, employ inductive coupling to transfer power and control signals wirelessly across the skin. Furthermore, SCS and DBS implantable pulse generators sometimes require communication with external modules for data transfer or recharging.

Wireless power transmission, battery recharging, and communication to implanted neural interfaces typically uses inductive radio frequency (RF) or near-field coils on either side of tissue (for example, skin (Zeng et al. [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR465)) or dura mater (Powell et al. [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR339))). The internal and external RF coils must be coupled by overlapping physically as much as possible with a small thickness of tissue in between; this may be difficult in some anatomical locations such as the skull or back (Troyk and Rush [2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR428)). The more electrodes in a neural interface the higher the power and data rate transmission requirements, increasing the bandwidth and power consumption (Nair et al. [2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR303)). Data and power transmission are limited due to safety restrictions: the power density in the body must be < 80 mW/cm 2 to avoid tissue damage from heating (Seese et al. [1998](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR368)).

Implanted electronics are packaged, typically in rigid Titanium housing (Sidambe [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR385)), which acts as a biocompatible hermetic seal. Hermetic seals keep the enclosed electronics safe and sound from the moisture and ions in the tissue (Merrill [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR285)). Wires connect the internal components to external components such as electrode leads via a feedthrough assembly that often consists of a ceramic or fused silica insulator (Nagarkar et al. [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR302)).

Lead wires connect the electrodes to the pulse generator and/or DAQ unit. They are insulated, often with silicone, polyimide, parylene, or other flexible inert polymer materials (Barrese et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR21); Kuo et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR243)).

Electrodes are the conductive materials that interface with neurons. They come in many forms including rings, pads, and shanks and can be penetrating or non-penetrating into the neural tissue (Fig.[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#Fig4)). Electrodes can vary greatly in size, depending on the neural target and electrode density, although electrodes on clinically available leads are more similar to each other. Some electrode designs, such as those for intraspinal microstimulation or longitudinal intrafascicular electrodes (LIFEs), use a continuous material for the lead wire and electrode, where the electrode is simply the de-insulated portion of the lead (Bamford et al. [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR18); Rijnbeek et al. [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR354)). Depth electrodes are similar in design to many clinically-available implanted electrodes; however, they come in a hybrid version where microwires splay from the tip of the array to record from single neurons (Fu and Rutishauser [2025](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR139)). Most clinical electrodes are comprised of platinum or platinum-iridium alloys (Cogan [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR76); Ford [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR135); Stöver and Lenarz [2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR400)). Pad-type electrodes, including those used for transverse intrafascicular multichannel electrodes (TIMEs) and ECoG, use sputtering, etching, stamping, or welding manufacturing techniques (Boretius et al. [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR37); Konerding et al. [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR229)). Many other electrodes, such as cylindrical electrodes or those used for intracortical interfaces, require metal bonding or welding (Barrese et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR21)). Coatings on the electrodes are sometimes used to increase the conductivity; commonly, iridium oxide is used (Woeppel et al. [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#CR455)).

Many implanted neural interfaces remain effective in humans for years to decades (Table[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11907899/#Tab1)). Despite the great successes of implanted neural interface systems, each of the aforementioned components is a potential source of failure. Furthermore, each application has specific challenges, such as surgical access, safe implantation, movement of local tissues, and electrochemical reactions at the electrode-tissue interface. The effectiveness and reliability of neural interfaces are limited by these technological and biological challenges that can impede translation and years-long use of the devices in humans. In the following sections, we will review several of these barriers and how they affect device longevity, with a focus on devices that have been implanted in humans. We will then highlight failure modes of devices during chronic in vivo implantation with specific examples reported from clinical use or clinical trials. We will also review failure modes associated with the development of novel implantable devices. With each failure mode, solutions arise to resolve the issues. We conclude with novel neural interfacing technologies and how they may be able to mitigate some of the issues experienced by traditional neural interface systems during long-term implantation.

Implantable brain [machine interfaces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-interface "Learn more about machine interfaces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (BMIs) are now on a trajectory to go mainstream, wherein what was once considered last resort will progressively become elective at earlier stages in disease treatment. First-in-human successes have demonstrated the ability to decode highly dexterous motor skills such as handwriting, and speech from human cortical activity. These have been used for cursor and prosthesis control, direct-to-text communication and speech synthesis. Along with these breakthrough studies, [technology advancements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/technology-advancement "Learn more about technology advancements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have enabled the observation of more channels of neural activity through new concepts for centralised/distributed implant architectures. This is complemented by research in [flexible substrates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/flexible-substrate "Learn more about flexible substrates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), packaging, surgical workflows and data processing. New regulatory guidance and funding has galvanised the field. This culmination of resource, efforts and capability is now attracting significant investment for BMI commercialisation. This paper reviews recent developments and describes the paradigm shift in BMI development that is leading to new innovations, insights and BMI translation.

Introduction
------------

Millions today are benefiting from modern [neurotechnology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurotechnology "Learn more about neurotechnology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") through devices such as [cochlear implants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cochlear-implant "Learn more about cochlear implants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), for hearing loss; [deep brain stimulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electrical-brain-stimulation "Learn more about deep brain stimulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DBS), for [movement disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/stereotypic-movement-disorder "Learn more about movement disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); and spinal cord [stimulators](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/stimulator "Learn more about stimulators from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), for chronic pain. There is an appetite in both the research community and industry to extend capability. This will improve effectiveness (e.g. increased efficacy, reduced side effects) but also tackle new applications (e.g. [visual prostheses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/visual-prosthesis "Learn more about visual prostheses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), paralysis, epilepsy, dementia, mental health).

[Brain computer interfaces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/brain-computer-interface "Learn more about Brain computer interfaces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (BCIs) and brain [machine interfaces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-interface "Learn more about machine interfaces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (BMIs) refer to neurotechnologies that observe activity within the brain and decode or decipher this to extract useful information. Although the terminology BCI and BMI are often used interchangeably, there is a tendency within the community to refer to non-invasive interfaces using BCI and implantable interfaces using BMI [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0005)]. There exists a plethora of methods to observe neural activity including electroencephalography (EEG), [magnetoencephalography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/magnetoencephalography "Learn more about magnetoencephalography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (MEG), [electrocorticography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electrocorticography "Learn more about electrocorticography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ECoG) also referred to as intracranial EEG, intracortical recording, functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), functional near infra-red spectroscopy (fNIRS), [positron emission tomography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/positron-emission-tomography "Learn more about positron emission tomography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PET), and others [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0010)]. From all these approaches it is widely accepted that implantable methods (e.g. ECoG, intracortical recording) outperform non-invasive methods (e.g. EEG, fNIRS) due to a significantly higher spatial and temporal resolution and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Moreover, although it is possible to observe activity using several different modalities (e.g. electrical, optical, magnetic) [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0015)], electrical recording is the most established and therefore best suited in the near term for emerging clinical devices [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0010)].

Particularly for implantable BMIs, recent advances in integrated circuit and microsystem technologies have enabled the observation of multiple channels of neural activity (e.g. electrophysiology) using low power, low noise and compact recording electronics. This has empowered the community to develop novel tools enabling scientific discovery [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0015), [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0020), [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0025), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0030),[7•](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0035)] but also translational efforts applying BMIs to clinical applications. This, for example, can enable communication for people with [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyotrophic-lateral-sclerosis "Learn more about amyotrophic lateral sclerosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ALS) or locked-in syndrome [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0040),[9••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0045),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0050),[11••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0055)], or provide a means for control of external prostheses or exoskeletons for people that are paralysed through a [spinal cord injury](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/spinal-cord-injury "Learn more about spinal cord injury from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SCI) [[12•](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0060),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0065)].

This review explores the latest progress specifically in intracortical BMIs, identifying the key challenges and emerging technology trends for next-generation devices. Key examples of current and emerging neural interface technologies are shown in [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#fig0005).

Implantable human BMIs today
----------------------------

Current translational efforts that are applying implantable [BMIs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/machine-interface "Learn more about BMIs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to clinical applications are only possible thanks to decades of research in basic and applied science using [animal models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/animal-model "Learn more about animal models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0115)]. This has informed key decisions such as [electrode types](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrode-type "Learn more about electrode types from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), target brain area, electronic architecture, processing strategies, as well as providing pilot data and essential [biocompatibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/biocompatibility "Learn more about biocompatibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and safety data. Implantable human BMIs have to date utilised two main categories of neural interfaces targeting the [motor cortex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/motor-cortex "Learn more about motor cortex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): [ECoG](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electrocorticography "Learn more about ECoG from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") grids (placed on the surface of the brain) and penetrating [microelectrode](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/microelectrode "Learn more about microelectrode from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") arrays (MEAs) (inserted into cortical tissue) [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0010)].

The first implantable BMI demonstrated in human was in 2004. This used an intracortical [MEA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrode-array "Learn more about MEA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (commonly known as the Utah electrode array, at the time developed by Cyberkinetics Inc.), implanted in a person with [tetraplegia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tetraplegia "Learn more about tetraplegia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") due to C3 [spinal cord injury](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/spinal-cord-injury "Learn more about spinal cord injury from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Cyberkinetics later spun off the device manufacturing to Blackrock Microsystems LLC and the clinical development to Braingate Inc.[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#fn0005) The Braingate2 trial has continued developing and [translating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/translating-language "Learn more about translating from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") prosthesis applications for communication using a cursor [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0040)] and most recently, handwriting control [[11••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0055)] as well as for movement restoration [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0120)]. Additionally, a number of trials independant of the braingate consortium (including Battelle/Ohio State University, CalTech, Johns Hopkins, Thomas Jefferson University, and the University of Pittsburgh) have utilised the Blackrock device in human BMIs. Although signal processing, applied [neuroscience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neuroscience "Learn more about neuroscience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and clinical validation have advanced significantly over the past 17 years, the hardware itself remains based on scientific instrumentation provided by Blackrock Microsystems LLC. The most recent innovation here has been the introduction of wireless, non-implantable telemetry [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0075)]. The neural interface itself (Utah electrode array, marketed under BlackRock Microsystem ‘NeuroPort’) to this day remains the only intracortical electrode array that has been approved for human use (FDA 510(k) cleared) for temporary (<30 day) monitoring of neural activity. All chronic implants are occurring under investigational device exemptions from the FDA led by each University.

A second implantable technology that has been used in human BMIs is [ECoG](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/electrocorticography "Learn more about ECoG from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This has the advantage of leveraging the availability of clinical-grade [ECoG](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/electrocorticography "Learn more about ECoG from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strips and grids used in routine [epilepsy surgery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/epilepsy-surgery "Learn more about epilepsy surgery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the associated surgical workflow (perioperative observation) as an opportunity for [clinical research](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/clinical-research "Learn more about clinical research from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0010),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0125)]. In the past decade, there have been several human studies advancing ECoG based BMIs. In 2016, Vansteensel _et al._ demonstrated the first chronic, fully-implantable BMI using ECoG in a patient with [ALS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyotrophic-lateral-sclerosis "Learn more about ALS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[14•](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0070)]. This used a closed-loop [DBS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electrical-brain-stimulation "Learn more about DBS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") device (Medtronic PC+S) that was repurposed to use 4× four-contact ECoG strips to observe the neural activity. This work was then extended to assess the long term stability of the ECoG BMI during home use for 3 years [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0050)]. Benabid _et al._ developed a bespoke [implantable device](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/medical-implant "Learn more about implantable device from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") integrating an 8×8 ECoG grid [[12•](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0060)]. This was applied to control a four-limb [neuroprosthetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neuroprosthetics "Learn more about neuroprosthetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") exoskeleton and tested over 2 years. Most recently, Herff _et al._ and Anumanchipalli _et al._ independently investigated utilising ECoG grids to observe activity in the speech [motor cortex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/motor-cortex "Learn more about motor cortex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") area and directly decode audio waveforms [[9••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0045),[26••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0130)]. This demonstrated that a different target (to cortical areas previously studied, for example, motor cortex) and approach could be used to extract information.

Technical challenges
--------------------

Extracting useful information from [brain activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/brain-activity "Learn more about brain activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") remains a very challenging and multifaceted problem. Key challenges with intracortical BMIs are overlapping and interrelated, compounding difficulty as they cannot be solved in isolation. The challenges can however be separated roughly into three categories, namely scalability and portability, electrode stability and yield, and information transfer rate, described below.

### Scalability and portability

Increasing the amount of available neural information has been one of the key recent objectives in the field of cortical BMIs. This effort has primarily been translated into the development of interfaces with higher channel counts [[16•](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0080),[27••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0135),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0140)], with some exploration of higher signal resolution and dimensionality. The purpose of increasing signal bit depth or resolution is primarily to better differentiate between action potentials captured from different neurons in the vicinity of an electrode [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0145)] when using [spike detection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/spike-detection "Learn more about spike detection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or sorting algorithms. In terms of increasing channel counts, the main issues are related to electrode interconnect with the implant [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0150)], and data telemetry limitations for transferring [neural data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/neural-data "Learn more about neural data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the skull and skin layers to an external storage and processing node. Intracortical implants have traditionally been limited by available power especially for wireless transmission [[27••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0135),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0145)], though more recently this issue has become more of a tradeoff between power assigned to radio transmission and space, complexity and power assigned to processing for compression of neural data before transmission [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0150)]. The fundamental limitation in power budgets for intracortical [BCIs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/brain-computer-interface "Learn more about BCIs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") lies in maximum [thermal dissipation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-dissipation "Learn more about thermal dissipation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which must limit any temperature increase of surrounding tissue to below 0.5°C [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0140)]. Additional constraints on coil size result in lower power transmission efficiencies and increased thermal dissipation, in addition to the challenges of transferring power across the skin and skull, which absorb RF energy readily in addition to placing constraints on the size of receiving coils due to large wavelengths [[27••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0135)]. A related challenge is also that of portability, that is, being able to integrate high channel count systems within a compact form factor.

### Electrode stability and yield

Related to accessing more neural information, is a BMI system's ability to retain access to that information over time. Traditional BMIs have been fundamentally limited by implant lifetime, neural signal non-stationarity, and electrode yield. Implant lifetime is defined as the period of time during which neurons in proximity to the electrodes remain viable. Neural signal non-stationarity refers to how neural signals encoding a specific output change over time, and electrode yield refers to whether an electrode is recording useful information in the use context of the BMI.

Implant lifetimes have so far reached a few years _in vivo_ without significant increases beyond this [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0155),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0160)]. This is related to the body's response to foreign objects with high [young's modulus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/youngs-modulus "Learn more about young's modulus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the brain [[27••](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0135),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0165), [34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0170), [35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S095816692100183X#bib0175)], as well as implantation [trauma](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/injury "Learn more about trauma from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as brain [bleeds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/ciclonicate "Learn more about bleeds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from cut capillaries or larger vessels, which causes [chronic inflammation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/chronic-inflammation "Learn more about chronic inflammation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leading to cell death and [fibrous encapsulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/fibrous-encapsulation "Learn more about fibrous encapsulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") around the electrode [[36](https://www.sciencedirec

Abstract
--------

Flexible deep brain neural interfaces, as an important research direction in the field of neural engineering, have broad application prospects in areas such as neural signal detection, treatment of neurological diseases, and intelligent control systems. However, chronic inflammatory responses caused by long-term implantation and the resulting electrode failure seriously hinder the clinical development of this technology. This review systematically explores the long-term stability issues of flexible deep brain neural interfaces, with a focus on analyzing the synergistic optimization of electrode geometric morphology and implantation strategies in regulating inflammatory responses. Additionally, this paper delves into innovative strategies, such as passive enhancement of biocompatibility through electrode surface functionalization and active inhibition of inflammation through drug-controlled release systems, offering new technical paths to extend electrode lifespan. By integrating and reviewing existing innovative methods for deep brain flexible electrodes, this study provides an important theoretical foundation and technical guidance for the development of high-stability neural interface devices.

Introduction
------------

In the new era of brain-machine interfaces, the long-term stability of deep brain flexible electrodes is a critical performance indicator for their application in treating neurological disorders and intelligent control[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR1 "Zhao, Z. et al. Ultraflexible electrode arrays for months-long high-density electrophysiological mapping of thousands of neurons in rodents. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 520–532 (2022)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR2 "Oganesian, L. L. & Shanechi, M. M. Brain–computer interfaces for neuropsychiatric disorders. Nat. Rev. Bioeng. 2, 653–670 (2024)."). Although flexible electrodes have a lower Young’s modulus compared to rigid electrodes, enhancing mechanical compatibility with brain tissue, they still inevitably trigger immune responses[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR3 "Tang, X., Shen, H., Zhao, S., Li, N. & Liu, J. Flexible brain–computer interfaces. Nat. Electron 6, 109–118 (2023)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR4 "Wang, J. et al. Flexible electrodes for brain–computer interface system. Adv. Mater. 35, 2211012 (2023)."). The immune response, a key factor affecting the long-term stability of electrodes, includes acute inflammatory reactions during implantation and chronic inflammation following implantation[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR5 "Rivnay, J., Wang, H., Fenno, L., Deisseroth, K. & Malliaras, G. G. Next-generation probes, particles, and proteins for neural interfacing. Sci. Adv. 3, e1601649 (2017)."). Acute inflammatory responses occur due to geometric and mechanical mismatches between the electrode and brain tissue during implantation[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR6 "Salatino, J. W., Ludwig, K. A., Kozai, T. D. Y. & Purcell, E. K. Glial responses to implanted electrodes in the brain. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 1, 862–877 (2017)."). This mismatch causes mechanical impact, tearing target neuronal tissue, damaging neurons and nerve fibers, and leading to tissue displacement and deformation. Injured tissue releases inflammatory factors that attract immune cells to the injured area to phagocytose cell debris and dead cells[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR7 "Skaper, S. D., Facci, L., Zusso, M. & Giusti, P. An inflammation-centric view of neurological disease: beyond the neuron. Front. Cell. Neurosci. 12, 72 (2018)."),[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR8 "Wu, N. et al. Electrode materials for brain–machine interface: a review. InfoMat 3, 1174–1194 (2021)."). Additionally, when electrode implantation pierces blood vessels, it triggers clotting and thrombus formation, attracting immune cells that release additional inflammatory factors, worsening the acute inflammatory response[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR9 "Shen, K., Chen, O., Edmunds, J. L., Piech, D. K. & Maharbiz, M. M. Translational opportunities and challenges of invasive electrodes for neural interfaces. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 424–442 (2023)."). Over time, the acute inflammatory response gradually diminishes, but the mechanical mismatch between the implant and brain tissue persists[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR9 "Shen, K., Chen, O., Edmunds, J. L., Piech, D. K. & Maharbiz, M. M. Translational opportunities and challenges of invasive electrodes for neural interfaces. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 424–442 (2023)."). Macroscopic and microscopic movements lead to friction between the implant and brain tissue, causing ongoing damage. This foreign body injury rapidly activates microglia, which release inflammatory cytokines and reactive oxygen species to clear the foreign material[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR10 "Wang, Y., Yang, X., Zhang, X., Wang, Y. & Pei, W. Implantable intracortical microelectrodes: reviewing the present with a focus on the future. Microsyst. Nanoeng. 9, 7 (2023)."),[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR11 "Lyu, J. et al. Microglial responses to brain injury and disease: functional diversity and new opportunities. Transl. Stroke Res. 12, 474–495 (2021)."). Meanwhile, astrocytes proliferate and migrate toward the injury site, secreting extracellular matrix (ECM) components and cytokines[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR12 "Oakes, R. S., Polei, M. D., Skousen, J. L. & Tresco, P. A. An astrocyte derived extracellular matrix coating reduces astrogliosis surrounding chronically implanted microelectrode arrays in rat cortex. Biomaterials 154, 1–11 (2018)."). ECM components accumulate around the foreign body, forming a dense physical barrier. Ultimately, glial cells proliferate and align at the injury site, forming a compact cell layer, which leads to persistent scar formation and eventually induces fibrosis[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR13 "He, Y., Liu, X. & Chen, Z. Glial scar—a promising target for improving outcomes after CNS injury. J. Mol. Neurosci. 70, 340–352 (2020)."). Scar tissue forms an insulating sheath around the electrode, increasing the distance between neurons and electrode sites, causing rapid signal attenuation and a sharp rise in impedance[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR10 "Wang, Y., Yang, X., Zhang, X., Wang, Y. & Pei, W. Implantable intracortical microelectrodes: reviewing the present with a focus on the future. Microsyst. Nanoeng. 9, 7 (2023)."). The insulation effect reduces electrode transmission efficiency while damaging the electrode’s electrochemical, electrical/optical stimulation, and electrophysiological recording functions, ultimately leading to failure[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR14 "Song, E., Li, J., Won, S. M., Bai, W. & Rogers, J. A. Materials for flexible bioelectronic systems as chronic neural interfaces. Nat. Mater. 19, 590–603 (2020)."). Additionally, micromovements of the electrode in brain tissue and surface conditions exacerbate the impact of chronic inflammatory responses[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR15 "Gulino, M., Kim, D., Pané, S., Santos, S. D. & Pêgo, A. P. Tissue response to neural implants: the use of model systems toward new design solutions of implantable microelectrodes. Front. Neurosci. 13, 689 (2019).").

Since the implantation method of neural interfaces directly affects the degree of immune response, and electrode material and shape determine the implantation method, the substrate of implantable electrodes has gradually shifted from rigid to flexible materials to minimize immune responses[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR9 "Shen, K., Chen, O., Edmunds, J. L., Piech, D. K. & Maharbiz, M. M. Translational opportunities and challenges of invasive electrodes for neural interfaces. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 424–442 (2023)."). Flexible materials reduce mechanical impact during implantation and relative motion with brain tissue post-implantation, making them more promising tools for neural interfaces[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR3 "Tang, X., Shen, H., Zhao, S., Li, N. & Liu, J. Flexible brain–computer interfaces. Nat. Electron 6, 109–118 (2023)."),[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR16 "Chen, Y. et al. How is flexible electronics advancing neuroscience research? Biomaterials 268, 120559 (2021)."). However, the shape design, implantation method, and surface modification of flexible electrodes still cannot meet the requirement for years-long detection periods[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR17 "Servais, B., Mahmoudi, N., Gautam, V. & Tong, W. Engineering brain-on-a-chip platforms. Nat. Rev. Bioeng. 2, 691–709 (2024)."). Therefore, summarizing strategies to further optimize flexible deep brain neural interfaces concerning immune response is urgently needed.

We believe that the shape of deep brain flexible neural interfaces determines the implantation method, which directly influences the extent of implantation-induced damage and the acute inflammatory response. The structural characteristics of the neural interface, as well as the geometric and mechanical mismatch between the implant and brain tissue, are the main factors that trigger chronic inflammatory responses. This chronic inflammation continues to affect the effective operational lifetime of the neural interface. Flexible electrodes have significantly mitigated the mechanical mismatch issues associated with traditional rigid interfaces, particularly in terms of Young’s modulus. The further goal of reducing immune responses is to make the electrodes “invisible” within neural tissue, passively “escaping” the immune system’s recognition, and thus aligning their shape and surface properties with the neural tissue. However, implantation inevitably causes some degree of brain tissue damage. Therefore, it is also crucial to enable the electrode to actively release anti-inflammatory substances to modulate the surrounding environment and promote tissue repair. Additionally, the compatibility between passive invisibility and active modulation strategies can maximize the electrode’s lifespan (Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1: Deep brain Flexible Neural Interface for Long-term Stability Strategies.**

[![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41528-025-00410-x/MediaObjects/41528_2025_410_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x/figures/1)

The long-term stability of flexible brain-machine interfaces requires consideration of implantation methods and surface modifications of neural interfaces. Implantation methods that are compatible with electrode shapes can reduce acute inflammatory responses during implantation, such as tungsten wire-guided insertion for filamentous electrodes, carrier-guided insertion for mesh electrodes, and direct insertion for Utah-like electrodes. Passive surface modifications of neural interfaces help them blend into the microenvironment to ensure functional stability, while active modifications can adjust the microenvironment to maintain surrounding neural bioactivity. Balancing shape, implantation, and modification ultimately extends the detection effectiveness and lifespan of neural interfaces.

Stability Prerequisite: Coordinated Electrode Shape and Implantation Method
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mechanical properties of brain tissue, such as its low Young’s modulus (approximately 1–10 kPa) and high compliance, pose significant challenges for electrode implantation. To ensure long-term stability and minimize tissue damage, the mechanical properties of the electrode must be carefully matched to those of the brain. Flexible electrodes, with their low bending stiffness and Young’s modulus, are designed to mimic the softness of brain tissue, reducing the risk of chronic inflammation and mechanical mismatch. However, their inherent flexibility limits their ability to penetrate brain tissue directly, necessitating the use of rigid shuttles or surface stiffness enhancement techniques for precise implantation (formulas 1 and 2)[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR18 "Lecomte, A., Descamps, E. & Bergaud, C. A review on mechanical considerations for chronically-implanted neural probes. J. Neural Eng. 15, 031001 (2018).").

$$Bending\\,stiffness=EI=E\\frac{\\pi {r}^{4}}{4}$$

(1)

$$Bending\\,stiffness=EI=E\\frac{b{h}^{3}}{12}$$

(2)

E represents Young’s modulus, and I is the moment of inertia. Formula (1) applies to circular cross-sections, with r as the cross-sectional radius. Formula (2) applies to rectangular cross-sections, with b as the width and h as the height. Flexible electrodes often utilize rigid shuttle guidance or surface stiffness enhancement techniques to enhance bending stiffness, ensuring precise implantation[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR19 "Seo, K. J. et al. A soft, high-density neuroelectronic array. npj Flex. Electron 7, 40 (2023)."). The choice of implantation method depends on the electrode’s shape. Simple-shaped electrodes, such as rod or filament-like electrodes, are compatible with various implantation schemes[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR20 "Hong, G. & Lieber, C. M. Novel electrode technologies for neural recordings. Nat. Rev. Neurosci. 20, 330–345 (2019)."). Complex-shaped electrodes, like mesh electrodes, require special attention to maintain stability during implantation[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR21 "Huang, Y. et al. Flexible electronic-photonic 3D integration from ultrathin polymer chiplets. npj Flex. Electron 8, 61 (2024)."),[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR22 "Hong, G., Yang, X., Zhou, T. & Lieber, C. M. Mesh electronics: a new paradigm for tissue-like brain probes. Curr. Opin. Neurobiol. 50, 33–41 (2018)."). The cross-sectional area of implantation directly affects the extent of acute injury. Therefore, selecting an implantation method that matches the electrode’s shape and optimizing the implantation cross-sectional area contribute to the long-term stability of electrode recordings.

### Customized Coordination of Electrode Shape and Implantation Method

When using a rigid shuttle for electrode guidance, the electrode directly contacts brain tissue, allowing for simultaneous recording of neural signals[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR23 "Mo, F. et al. Single-neuron detection of place cells remapping in short-term memory using motion microelectrode arrays. Biosens. Bioelectron. 217, 114726 (2022)."). During the implantation process, the shape and amplitude of spike signals assist in determining the implantation site. Common guided implantation methods include tungsten wire[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR1 "Zhao, Z. et al. Ultraflexible electrode arrays for months-long high-density electrophysiological mapping of thousands of neurons in rodents. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 520–532 (2022)."), SU8[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR21 "Huang, Y. et al. Flexible electronic-photonic 3D integration from ultrathin polymer chiplets. npj Flex. Electron 8, 61 (2024)."), and injection[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR24 "Yang, X. et al. Bioinspired neuron-like electronics. Nat. Mater. 18, 510–517 (2019)."). Tungsten wire guidance is typically used for rod-like or filament-like electrodes due to its simplicity and speed[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR1 "Zhao, Z. et al. Ultraflexible electrode arrays for months-long high-density electrophysiological mapping of thousands of neurons in rodents. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 520–532 (2022)."). In contrast, SU8 guidance and injection pump technology are more suitable for complex electrodes, such as mesh electrodes[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR21 "Huang, Y. et al. Flexible electronic-photonic 3D integration from ultrathin polymer chiplets. npj Flex. Electron 8, 61 (2024)."). This is because the shape of complex electrodes needs to be preserved to the greatest extent possible to maintain the specific locations of functional sites.

#### Rod and Filament Electrodes: Coordinated Shape Design and Tungsten Wire Guidance

The classification of rod-like and filament-like electrodes is based on the maximum thickness and width of the implant shank[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR1 "Zhao, Z. et al. Ultraflexible electrode arrays for months-long high-density electrophysiological mapping of thousands of neurons in rodents. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 7, 520–532 (2022)."). Rod-like electrodes typically range from several hundred micrometers to millimeters, while filament-like electrodes range from submicron to tens of micrometers. A tungsten wire with a stepped tip passes through the guiding hole at the electrode tip and is fixed with a polyethylene glycol (PEG) coating (Fig. [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#Fig2))[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-025-00410-x#ref-CR25 "Du, Z. J. et al. Ultrasoft microwire neural electrodes improve chronic tissue integration. Acta Biomater. 53, 46–58 (2017)."). The electrode is guided into the target brain region. After the PEG melts, the tungsten wire is retracted.

Abstract
--------

The key to designing an implantable device lies in condensing the synergistic effects of diagnostic and therapeutic methods in a single tool. In conjunction with the integration of electrophysiology and optical modalities, a transparent neural interface alleviates challenges of conventional metal-based microelectrodes. In this review, the multimodal sensing and stimulation functionalities of recent research are addressed. Next, issues that arise when combining functionalities of conventional metal-based, opaque electrode arrays together with optical modalities—(1) photoelectric artifacts, (2) optical image blocking, and (3) light transmission efficiency—are introduced. Then, an introduction of advancing material candidates for transparent neural interfaces follows with the latest research.

Protoc. 11, 2201–2222 (2016)."),[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR17 "Yang, W. & Yuste, R. In vivo imaging of neural activity. Nat. Methods 14, 349–359 (2017)."). Even when applying optogenetics to treat neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease, epilepsy, or depression, is applied to an implantable device, serious electrophysiological signal contamination due to photoelectric artifacts occurs if optical transparency is not guaranteed[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR18 "Seo, J.-W. et al. Artifact-free 2D mapping of neural activity in vivo through transparent gold nanonetwork array. Adv. Funct. Mater. 30, 2000896 (2020)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR19 "Kozai, T. D. & Vazquez, A. L. Photoelectric artefact from optogenetics and imaging on microelectrodes and bioelectronics: new challenges and opportunities. J. Mater. Chem. B 3, 4965–4978 (2015)."),[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR20 "Wang, L.-C. et al. The use of a double-layer platinum black-conducting polymer coating for improvement of neural recording and mitigation of photoelectric artifact. Biosens. Bioelectron. 145, 111661 (2019)."),[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR21 "Pisanello, F., Sileo, L. & De Vittorio, M. Micro-and nanotechnologies for optical neural interfaces. Front. Neurosci. 10, 70 (2016)."). Transparent implantable microelectrode arrays (MEAs) can simultaneously acquire high-resolution electrophysiological signals with negligible difference in performance from conventional electrodes while alleviating fundamental problems of optical modality[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR22 "Araki, T. et al. Long-term implantable, flexible, and transparent neural interface based on Ag/Au core–shell nanowires. Adv. Healthc. Mater. 8, 1900130 (2019)."),[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR23 "Araki, T. et al. Wireless monitoring using a stretchable and transparent sensor sheet containing metal nanowires. Adv. Mater. 32, 1902684 (2020)."),[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR24 "Kuzum, D. et al. Transparent and flexible low noise graphene electrodes for simultaneous electrophysiology and neuroimaging. Nat. Commun. 5, 1–10 (2014)."),[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR25 "Qiang, Y. et al. Transparent arrays of bilayer-nanomesh microelectrodes for simultaneous electrophysiology and two-photon imaging in the brain. Sci. Adv. 4, eaat0626 (2018)."),[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR26 "Kim, K., Park, Y. G., Hyun, B. G., Choi, M. & Park, J. U. Recent advances in transparent electronics with stretchable forms. Adv. Mater. 31, 1804690 (2019)."),[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR27 "Zhang, J. et al. Stretchable transparent electrode arrays for simultaneous electrical and optical interrogation of neural circuits in vivo. Nano Lett. 18, 2903–2911 (2018)."). Unlike metal based opaque MEAs, transparent electrodes eliminate the drawbacks of multimodal systems in that they can be used to measure electrical signals from the brain in real time without optical interference through material and structural controls. Transparent, implantable electrodes are fabricated with carbon-based materials[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR27 "Zhang, J. et al. Stretchable transparent electrode arrays for simultaneous electrical and optical interrogation of neural circuits in vivo. Nano Lett. 18, 2903–2911 (2018)."),[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR28 "Park, D.-W. et al. Graphene-based carbon-layered electrode array technology for neural imaging and optogenetic applications. Nat. Commun. 5, 5258 (2014)."), conductive polymers[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR29 "Kang, H., Jung, S., Jeong, S., Kim, G. & Lee, K. Polymer-metal hybrid transparent electrodes for flexible electronics. Nat. Commun. 6, 1–7 (2015)."),[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR30 "Hofmann, A. I., Cloutet, E. & Hadziioannou, G. Materials for transparent electrodes: from metal oxides to organic alternatives. Adv. Electron. Mater. 4, 1700412 (2018)."),[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR31 "Green, R. & Abidian, M. R. Conducting polymers for neural prosthetic and neural interface applications. Adv. Mater. 27, 7620–7637 (2015)."), and metal nanowires[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR32 "Ye, S., Rathmell, A. R., Chen, Z., Stewart, I. E. & Wiley, B. J. Metal nanowire networks: the next generation of transparent conductors. Adv. Mater. 26, 6670–6687 (2014)."),[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR33 "Song, C.-H., Han, C. J., Ju, B.-K. & Kim, J.-W. Photoenhanced patterning of metal nanowire networks for fabrication of ultraflexible transparent devices. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8, 480–489 (2016)."),[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR34 "Jung, E. D. et al. Highly efficient flexible optoelectronic devices using metal nanowire-conducting polymer composite transparent electrode. Electron. Mater. Lett. 11, 906–914 (2015)."), which are primarily exploited in flexible electronics. In addition, transparent electrodes can be obtained by the geometrical design of materials for the electrodes. For instance, in the case of opaque metal, it is possible to obtain optical transparency by forming mesh-like or porous structures on the active materials, while also maintaining electrical properties like that of a conventional metal film[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR35 "Schneider, J. et al. Electrohydrodynamic nanodrip printing of high aspect ratio metal grid transparent electrodes. Adv. Funct. Mater. 26, 833–840 (2016)."),[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR36 "Gao, T. et al. Hierarchical graphene/metal grid structures for stable, flexible transparent conductors. ACS Nano 9, 5440–5446 (2015)."),[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR37 "Park, J. H. et al. Flexible and transparent metallic grid electrodes prepared by evaporative assembly. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 6, 12380–12387 (2014)."). Furthermore, to overcome the difficulty of securing both high electrical conductivity and optical transmittance, recent studies are focused on overcoming the disadvantages by mixing two or more materials[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR29 "Kang, H., Jung, S., Jeong, S., Kim, G. & Lee, K. Polymer-metal hybrid transparent electrodes for flexible electronics. Nat. Commun. 6, 1–7 (2015)."),[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR38 "Ginting, R. T., Ovhal, M. M. & Kang, J.-W. A novel design of hybrid transparent electrodes for high performance and ultra-flexible bifunctional electrochromic-supercapacitors. Nano Energy 53, 650–657 (2018)."),[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR39 "Won, Y. et al. Annealing-free fabrication of highly oxidation-resistive copper nanowire composite conductors for photovoltaics. NPG Asia Mater. 6, e105 (2014)."). Figure [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#Fig1) shows the schematic overview of this paper. In this review, electrophysiological/optical diagnosis and treatment modalities are discussed, and the latest studies integrating these two approaches are introduced. Next, issues that arise when conventional opaque implantable electrode arrays are applied to optogenetics and optical imaging—photoelectric artifacts, optical image blocking, and less light transmission are the cases. Finally, a method for fabricating a transparent electrode that can solve these problems is presented by subdividing it into two categories: from a material point of view and from a structural point of view. As for the material approach of fabricating transparent neural electrodes, an introduction of intrinsically transparent material candidates, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNT), conductive hydrogel, oxides, and conductive polymers, in addition to the latest research are discussed. Lastly, an introduction to research applied to biomedical engineering for a transparent electrode through a special structure is examined. This review consequently suggests the importance of transparent implantable devices for biomedical engineering and medical applications as a next-generation multimodal system for future research.

Electrical & optical modalities
-------------------------------

Recently, implantable neural devices that can identify complex neuronal circuits and diagnose and treat neurodegenerative diseases have been extensively studied[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR40 "Park, Y., Chung, T. S., Lee, G. & Rogers, J. A. Materials chemistry of neural interface technologies and recent advances in three-dimensional systems. Chem. Rev. 122, 5277–5316 (2022)."),[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR41 "Wellman, S. M. et al. A materials roadmap to functional neural interface design. Adv. Funct. Mater. 28, 1701269 (2018)."),[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR42 "Fattahi, P., Yang, G., Kim, G. & Abidian, M. R. A review of organic and inorganic biomaterials for neural interfaces. Adv. Mater. 26, 1846–1885 (2014)."). Among them, the electrophysiological approach is a representative method for analyzing neuro-dynamics and neurological disorders[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR43 "Ferguson, M., Sharma, D., Ross, D. & Zhao, F. A critical review of microelectrode arrays and strategies for improving neural interfaces. Adv. Healthc. Mater. 8, 1900558 (2019)."),[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR44 "Patil, A. C. & Thakor, N. V. Implantable neurotechnologies: a review of micro-and nanoelectrodes for neural recording. Med Biol. Eng. Comput 54, 23–44 (2016)."). The biggest advantage of an implantable device for electrophysiological recording is that it exhibits high temporal resolution as it can be exploited to measure biological signals in real time[45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR45 "Lee, W. et al. Integration of organic electrochemical and field-effect transistors for ultraflexible, high temporal resolution electrophysiology arrays. Adv. Mater. 28, 9722–9728 (2016)."). This property is advantageous for pathological diagnosis due to its potential for sophisticated analysis of signal transmission, even from a single neuron[46](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR46 "Fu, T.-M. et al. Stable long-term chronic brain mapping at the single-neuron level. Nat. Methods 13, 875–882 (2016)."). However, the methods of inserting devices into the brain or other organs are spatially limited because they can be operated only at the implant site. From an electrophysiological point of view, ‘spatial resolution’ varies greatly depending upon the size and spacing of electrodes for measuring neural activity. Especially, size of the electrophysiological sensing site is a significantly important factor in the performance and sensitivity of the neural interface. For example, although it has an advantage of being able to measure the activity of individual neurons in proximity with a small recording site (<1000 um 2), it is unavoidable from degradation of signal quality due to an increased electrochemical impedance. Therefore, for high spatial resolution electrophysiological recording, the size of the electrode densely distributed on a tissue should be reduced as much as possible to increase selectivity maintaining a low impedance value[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR41 "Wellman, S. M. et al. A materials roadmap to functional neural interface design. Adv. Funct. Mater. 28, 1701269 (2018)."). In other words, a device without empty measurement sites with dense electrodes in the same implanted area has high spatial resolution. In the case of a passive electrode array composed of one channel per one conductive interconnect, dense arrangement of recording channels is difficult due to wiring spatial charge for signal transmission. This is the reason that the spatial resolution of the electrophysiological approach is lower than that of the optical approaches[47](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR47 "He, B., Yang, L., Wilke, C. & Yuan, H. Electrophysiological imaging of brain activity and connectivity-challenges and opportunities. IEEE Trans. Biomed. Eng. 58, 1918–1931 (2011)."). To overcome this weakness, as shown in Fig. [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#Fig2), devices comprised of high-density, multi-channel electrode arrays are being thoroughly studied[48](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR48 "Chiang, C.-H. et al. Development of a neural interface for high-definition, long-term recording in rodents and nonhuman primates. Sci. Transl. Med. 12, eaay4682 (2020)."),[49](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41528-022-00178-4#ref-CR49 "Jun, J. J. et al. Fully integrated silicon probes for high-density recording of neural activity. Nature 551, 232–236 (2017)."). However, it is not entirely free from spatial constraints compared to optical approaches, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), or positron emission tomography (PET), which structurally analyze the brain or target organs.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Implanted neural interfaces, including cochlear implants and deep brain stimulation, demonstrate long-term efficacy, significantly improving patients' quality of life by restoring functions and alleviating symptoms.
Studies indicate high patient satisfaction with implanted neural interfaces, particularly in cochlear and spinal cord stimulators, reflecting their impact on daily living and overall well-being.
Long-term outcomes for implanted neural interfaces reveal durability, with many devices functioning effectively for years, though challenges like immune response and device encapsulation persist.
